task: Security review: check for SQL injection in any raw f-string queries, missing html.escape on user input, exposed secrets in git-tracked files, any endpoints missing auth that should have it.

assignments.backend: SQL injection audit: scan src/indiestack/db.py and all files in src/indiestack/routes/ for raw f-string SQL queries where user input is interpolated directly instead of using parameterized queries (?-placeholders). Also audit all route handlers for endpoints that modify data or access sensitive info but don't check request.state.user — compare against auth patterns in auth.py. Report every instance found with file path, line number, and severity.
assignments.frontend: XSS audit: scan all files in src/indiestack/routes/ for user-controlled data (query params, form data, URL path params, database fields from user submissions like tool names/descriptions/taglines) rendered in f-string HTML templates without html.escape(). Check components.py tool_card and page_shell for unescaped fields. Report every instance with file path, line number, the variable involved, and severity.
assignments.devops: Secrets audit: check git-tracked files for exposed secrets — scan for API keys, tokens, passwords, database credentials, Stripe keys, OAuth secrets, SMTP passwords in all tracked files (not just current tree — check git log for any secrets that were ever committed). Review .gitignore to confirm sensitive files (*.env, credentials, stacks.py, plans/) are excluded. Check Dockerfile and fly.toml for hardcoded secrets. Report any findings with file path and remediation steps.

decomposition.reasoning: All three security audit streams are independent and can run concurrently. Backend handles SQL injection (db layer expertise) and auth gaps (needs to understand route protection patterns). Frontend handles XSS/html.escape (template rendering expertise). DevOps handles secrets exposure (git and infrastructure expertise). No sequential dependencies between these checks.

strategy_review.reasoning: Security audit is one of the most defensible uses of agent time. The codebase uses raw f-string HTML templates (no auto-escaping engine) and raw SQL — both are high-risk patterns. Public GitHub repo means any committed secret is already exposed. With ~53 users and real Stripe payments flowing through, a SQL injection or XSS exploit is existential risk, not theoretical. No direct revenue path, but this protects the revenue that exists. The three-department split is clean with no overlap: backend owns SQL/auth, frontend owns XSS, devops owns secrets. Each is asked to report concrete findings with file paths and line numbers — actionable output, not busywork.

approved_tasks.backend: SQL injection audit: scan src/indiestack/db.py and all files in src/indiestack/routes/ for raw f-string SQL queries where user input is interpolated directly instead of using parameterized queries (?-placeholders). Also audit all route handlers for endpoints that modify data or access sensitive info but don't check request.state.user — compare against auth patterns in auth.py. Report every instance found with file path, line number, and severity.
approved_tasks.frontend: XSS audit: scan all files in src/indiestack/routes/ for user-controlled data (query params, form data, URL path params, database fields from user submissions like tool names/descriptions/taglines) rendered in f-string HTML templates without html.escape(). Check components.py tool_card and page_shell for unescaped fields. Report every instance with file path, line number, the variable involved, and severity.
approved_tasks.devops: Secrets audit: check git-tracked files for exposed secrets — scan for API keys, tokens, passwords, database credentials, Stripe keys, OAuth secrets, SMTP passwords in all tracked files. Review .gitignore to confirm sensitive files (*.env, credentials, stacks.py, plans/) are excluded. Check Dockerfile and fly.toml for hardcoded secrets. For git history, do a TARGETED search using pattern-matching (grep for key prefixes like sk_live, sk_test, password=, SECRET, SMTP, API_KEY across recent git log -p — cap at last 200 commits to control cost). Report any findings with file path and remediation steps.

conditions: DevOps git history scan MUST be targeted pattern-matching (grep for secret-shaped strings), NOT exhaustive manual review of every commit. Cap at last 200 commits to control agent cost.
All three departments must report findings in a consistent format: file path, line number, severity (critical/high/medium/low), and recommended fix.
If any CRITICAL findings emerge (live secrets exposed, confirmed SQL injection with user-reachable input), flag immediately — don't just include in a final report.
risk_flags: Git history scan could burn significant agent time if not scoped — the modified devops task caps this at 200 commits with pattern matching.

False positives are likely — not every f-string SQL query is injectable (some may only interpolate server-controlled values). Departments should distinguish between 'user input reaches this query' vs 'only server-controlled values'.
This audit may surface uncomfortable findings that need immediate remediation — be prepared to prioritize fixes in the next sprint.
